agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA display from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This affects how delivery commitments are communicated to customers and potentially how SLAs are measured, operations are planned, and partner integrations function.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — the projected delivery date/time communicated to the customer and used internally for routing and SLA compliance."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"The time range within which a delivery is promised. Currently an exact timestamp; proposed change makes this a full calendar day."},{"term":"SLA","definition":"Service Level Agreement — the contractual or advertised delivery commitment. Exact-timestamp ETAs imply hour-level SLAs; date-only ETAs imply day-level SLAs."},{"term":"Last Mile","definition":"The final delivery leg from distribution hub to recipient. Most time-sensitive and variable portion of the delivery chain."},{"term":"Delivery Commitment","definition":"The promise made to the customer about when their package will arrive. Changing ETA granularity directly changes the nature of this commitment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A delivery ETA must always be communicated to the customer after order confirmation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Customers must know when to expect delivery; omitting ETA breaks the fundamental order-fulfillment contract.","testable_assertion":"Every confirmed order has a non-null ETA visible to the customer."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"The ETA display granularity must be consistent across all customer-facing channels (app, email, SMS, web tracking).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Inconsistent ETAs across channels erode trust and create support escalations. A customer seeing '14:30' in email but 'Friday' in-app perceives a broken system.","testable_assertion":"Given a delivery with ETA, when viewed on any customer-facing channel, the ETA format and value are identical."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Internal operational systems (routing, dispatch, driver assignment) must continue to use precise timestamps regardless of customer-facing display.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Dispatch and routing optimization require minute-level precision. Degrading internal precision would collapse operational efficiency.","testable_assertion":"Internal dispatch records retain full timestamp precision (date + time) even when customer-facing display shows date-only."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"SLA compliance measurement must align with the communicated ETA granularity.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"SLA measurement policy can be adjusted by business leadership, but must be explicitly decided — not accidentally changed by a UI modification.","testable_assertion":"If ETA shows 'Friday', a delivery at 23:59 on Friday is SLA-compliant; if ETA shows '14:30 Friday', a delivery at 14:31 is non-compliant."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Premium/express service tiers may require more granular ETAs than standard tiers.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Business policy decision — express customers paying for time-definite delivery expect and are contractually entitled to time-level precision.","testable_assertion":"Given a premium service tier order, the ETA display format must include time, not just date."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer has a confirmed standard-tier delivery","when":"They view the delivery tracking page","then":"The ETA is displayed as a date-only window (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday') with no time component."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A delivery is made at any time on the ETA date","when":"SLA compliance is evaluated","then":"The delivery is marked as on-time regardless of the hour of delivery."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An internal dispatcher views the same delivery","when":"They access the dispatch or routing system","then":"The full timestamp ETA is visible for operational planning."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A customer views ETA on the mobile app and then checks email notification","when":"Both displays are rendered","then":"Both show the same date-only format with identical date values."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"The ETA date rolls over (e.g., delay pushes delivery from Friday to Monday)","when":"The customer is notified of the change","then":"A proactive notification is sent with the updated date-only ETA and a reason for the change."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A delivery is associated with a premium or time-definite service tier","when":"The ETA is displayed to the customer","then":"The ETA retains time-level granularity (not date-only), preserving the premium commitment."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Delivery scheduled for end-of-day Friday but actually arrives Saturday at 00:05 due to timezone or slight delay.","expected_behavior":"System must define a clear cutoff (e.g., 23:59:59 in recipient's local timezone) for what counts as 'Friday'. A 00:05 Saturday delivery is late."},{"scenario":"Customer in a different timezone than the distribution center sees 'Delivery by Friday' — whose Friday?","expected_behavior":"ETA date must be calculated and displayed in the recipient's local timezone, not the warehouse or server timezone."},{"scenario":"Multiple parcels in one order have different ETAs — one is Friday, another is Monday.","expected_behavior":"Each parcel shows its own date-only ETA. The order-level ETA should show the latest date or clearly indicate split delivery."},{"scenario":"Business customer with contractual SLA requiring time-definite delivery receives date-only ETA.","expected_behavior":"Contractual SLA customers must be excluded from the date-only change. Their ETAs must remain time-precise per contract terms."},{"scenario":"ETA date is today — 'Delivery by today' is ambiguous about urgency.","expected_behavior":"Same-day ETAs should include a time window or qualifier (e.g., 'Delivery today by end of day') to set expectations."},{"scenario":"API consumers (partners, marketplaces) currently parse exact timestamps from tracking APIs.","expected_behavior":"API response schema must continue returning full timestamps. Display-layer change must not break API contracts."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Reducing ETA precision lowers the SLA compliance bar — deliveries arriving anytime on the promised date count as on-time. This could reduce refund/credit payouts for 'late' deliveries by 10-30% depending on current miss rates at the hour level. However, it may also reduce competitive differentiation against carriers offering precise windows, potentially impacting customer acquisition and retention for time-sensitive segments.","operational":"Significant reduction in customer support contacts. Exact-timestamp misses (e.g., arriving at 14:45 when ETA was 14:30) generate complaints even though the package arrived the same day. Date-only windows absorb intra-day variability, reducing 'where is my package' calls. Operations teams gain flexibility in route optimization since any delivery time on the target date satisfies the commitment. Risk: teams may deprioritize on-time performance since the bar is lower.","user":"Mixed impact. Customers who rarely track deliveries won't notice. Customers who plan their day around deliveries (e.g., work-from-home to receive a package) lose critical information. B2B customers with receiving dock schedules are severely impacted — they need time-level precision to staff receiving. Net Promoter Score impact depends heavily on customer segment mix.","risk":"1) Contractual risk: B2B and premium customers may have SLAs requiring time-definite ETAs — blanket removal could breach contracts. 2) Competitive risk: Amazon, FedEx, UPS all trend toward MORE precise windows, not less. Moving opposite to market direction is a strategic risk. 3) Regression risk: if internal systems also lose precision (not just display), route optimization degrades. 4) Regulatory risk: some jurisdictions require delivery time specificity for certain goods (perishables, medical).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End consumers (standard tier)","impact":"Lose time-level visibility. Most won't care; time-sensitive recipients will be frustrated. Fewer 'late delivery' complaints for intra-day misses."},{"group":"End consumers (premium/express tier)","impact":"CRITICAL — must be excluded from this change. They pay for time-definite service. Removing time precision breaks the value proposition."},{"group":"B2B / Enterprise customers","impact":"HIGH — receiving docks, warehouse staff scheduling, and downstream production depend on time-precise ETAs. Date-only is operationally insufficient."},{"group":"Customer support team","impact":"POSITIVE — fewer 'late by minutes' complaints. But new category of complaints: 'I waited all day and it came at 8 PM.'"},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must retain internal time precision. If change is display-only, minimal impact. If it propagates to operational systems, severe degradation."},{"group":"Partner / Marketplace integrations","impact":"API consumers parsing timestamps will break if schema changes. Must maintain backward-compatible API responses."},{"group":"Product / Marketing","impact":"Messaging shift required. Current marketing may promise precise ETAs. Competitive positioning affected."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The proposal is described at a high level without specifying: (a) which service tiers are affected, (b) whether this is display-only or also changes SLA measurement, (c) whether API contracts change, (d) the business motivation driving this change. These ambiguities prevent confident assessment of whether the change is domain-correct or harmful.","high_confidence_areas":["Internal systems must retain full timestamp precision regardless of display changes","Multi-channel ETA consistency is a hard requirement","Premium/express tiers likely need exclusion from date-only display","API backward compatibility must be preserved"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether premium and B2B tiers are in scope or excluded","Whether SLA measurement methodology changes alongside display","The business motivation — is this to reduce SLA misses, simplify UX, or reduce computational cost of precise ETAs","Whether same-day delivery ETAs are also changed to date-only","Regulatory requirements for ETA precision in specific verticals or jurisdictions"],"assumptions":["This is a customer-facing display change, not an internal operational change","The underlying ETA calculation engine continues to produce precise timestamps","Standard parcel delivery is the primary use case, not perishables or medical"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The proposal does not specify which service tiers are affected. Applying date-only ETAs to premium/express tiers would violate the value proposition of time-definite service. Does this change apply to all tiers or only standard?","affected_stakeholders":["Premium/express customers","Product team","Sales/contracts team"],"options":["Apply to standard tier only, preserve time-precision for premium/express","Apply universally across all tiers","Apply to standard tier now, evaluate premium tier separately"],"recommendation":"Apply to standard tier only. Premium and express customers pay for time-definite delivery; removing that precision breaks the service contract."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"It is unclear whether 'change ETAs to date-only' is a display-layer change or also modifies SLA compliance measurement. These are fundamentally different changes with very different business impact.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations team","Finance/billing","Customer support","Legal/contracts"],"options":["Display-only change: show date-only to customers, measure SLA internally against full timestamps","Display + SLA change: both show and measure against date-only windows","Display change with explicit SLA policy review before aligning measurement"],"recommendation":"Treat as display-only initially. SLA measurement methodology is a business policy decision requiring explicit sign-off from operations, finance, and legal — it must not change implicitly as a side effect of a UI change."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The proposal does not address API consumers. Tracking APIs currently return precise timestamps. Changing the API response format to date-only would break partner and marketplace integrations.","affected_stakeholders":["Partner integrations","Marketplace channels","Engineering team"],"options":["API continues to return full timestamps; only UI changes","API adds a new field for date-only alongside existing timestamp field","API changes to date-only with deprecation period for old format"],"recommendation":"API must continue returning full timestamps. Display formatting is a presentation concern, not a data model concern. Breaking API contracts is a separate, much higher-risk change."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No clarity on the business motivation for this change. Is it to reduce SLA miss rates, simplify the user experience, reduce engineering complexity of precise ETA calculation, or something else? The motivation determines whether this is a good trade-off.","affected_stakeholders":["Product team","Engineering","Executive leadership"],"options":["Motivated by reducing false SLA misses — suggests SLA policy is the real problem","Motivated by UX simplification — may be valid if research shows users prefer date-only","Motivated by engineering cost — suggests ETA precision is expensive to maintain"],"recommendation":"Clarify motivation before proceeding. If the goal is reducing SLA misses, fix SLA policy directly rather than hiding precision. If UX-driven, validate with user research. If engineering-driven, the business cost of losing precision may outweigh engineering savings."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this business domain change for delivery ETA display format.

This is a clear domain analysis task about changing ETA precision from exact timestamps to date-only windows. Let me think through this carefully.

Classification: This is an `ambiguity` situation. The proposal "change to date-only windows" is vague — it doesn't specify why this change is being made, what problem it solves, whether SLAs are affected, whether this is UI-only or affects contractual commitments, what happens to existing integrations, etc. Multiple valid interpretations exist.

Let me produce the full analysis.
